messages.content: Hi! Can you please add two details to the system prompt or whatever explains replace: first, that it's a pure inline xml call, not a tool call; second, that there is a bias towards saying [replace][old][/old][new][/old][/replace] for some reason and could the model please look out for that?
I'll find the system prompt file and add those two clarifications about `<replace>`.
{"action": "update_context", "add": ["forge/prompts/system.py"], "message": "Added 1 files, removed 0 files from context", "remove": [], "success": true}

I'll add both clarifications to the `<replace>` documentation in the EDIT_FORMAT_XML section.

<replace_zq7 file="forge/prompts/system.py">
<old_zq7>
### Surgical Edits with `<replace>`

To change part of a file, find the exact text and replace it:

```
<replace file="path/to/file.py">
<old>
exact text to find
</old>
<new>
replacement text
</new>
</replace>
```

Rules:
- The `<old>` text must match EXACTLY (whitespace, indentation, every character)
- Only the first occurrence is replaced
- You can include multiple `<replace>` blocks in one response
- Edits are applied in order; if one fails, later edits are skipped
- An empty `<new>` block deletes the matched text
</old_zq7>
<new_zq7>
### Surgical Edits with `<replace>`

`<replace>` is a **pure inline XML command** that you write directly in your
response prose. It is **not** an API tool call — do not put it inside a
`<function_calls>` block, do not invoke it via `update_context` or any other
function. Just write the XML inline and the parser will pick it up.

To change part of a file, find the exact text and replace it:

```
<replace file="path/to/file.py">
<old>
exact text to find
</old>
<new>
replacement text
</new>
</replace>
```

Rules:
- The `<old>` text must match EXACTLY (whitespace, indentation, every character)
- Only the first occurrence is replaced
- You can include multiple `<replace>` blocks in one response
- Edits are applied in order; if one fails, later edits are skipped
- An empty `<new>` block deletes the matched text

### ⚠️ Common Mistake: Wrong Closing Tag on `<new>`

For some reason there is a strong bias toward writing the closing tag of the
`<new>` block as `</old>` instead of `</new>`, producing malformed structure
like:

```
<replace file="...">
<old>
...
</old>
<new>
...
</old>      ← WRONG! This must be </new>
</replace>
```

**Before sending any `<replace>` block, glance at the four inner tags and
confirm the pattern is exactly:** `<old>` … `</old>` … `<new>` … `</new>`.
Mismatched closers will cause the edit to fail to parse.
</new_zq7>
</replace_zq7>

Done. Added two clarifications to the `<replace>` docs in `forge/prompts/system.py`:

1. An explicit note up front that `<replace>` is a pure inline XML command, not an API tool call — it shouldn't go inside `<function_calls>`.
2. A "Common Mistake" callout warning about the bias toward closing `<new>` with `</old>`, with a visual of the wrong pattern and a reminder to verify the `<old>…</old> <new>…</new>` structure before sending.

I used the nonced form (`_zq7`) for the edit because the body contains literal `</old>`, `</new>`, and `</replace>` tokens.

thanks that worked perfectly. some notes: um... the model can't exactly "glance before sending". it's always sending. it has to pay attention when writing the closing new tag to begin with. second, I note that you said "invoke via update_context". I have noticed before that there's a strong bias towards using update_context when you're not sure how to do something, why would that be? update_context has nothing to do with tool calls. is it too close to an existing tool call that you know that we don't support?

[id 6] Both great points. Let me fix the "glance before sending" framing and think about the `update_context` bias.

On the first point you're absolutely right — there's no "before sending" moment for me. The advice has to land while writing the closing tag, not retroactively. Let me rewrite that.

On the second point, your hypothesis seems plausible. `update_context` is the most-used and most-recently-trained-on tool name in this codebase, and when I'm reaching for "the generic way to do a thing with files," it's the lexically nearest handle. It's similar in shape to tools I've seen in other harnesses (`read_file`, `open_file`, `view`, etc.) and to things like `str_replace_editor` / `text_editor` where one tool covers many file ops. So when the actual mechanism is unfamiliar (an inline XML command), the prior pulls toward "must be `update_context`" as the path of least resistance — even though semantically it only loads files into context. It's a wrong-but-confident substitution. Fixing the prompt to call out that specific mistake (and not just say "don't use a function") should help future-me notice.

<replace_mn4 file="forge/prompts/system.py">
<old_mn4>
### Surgical Edits with `<replace>`

`<replace>` is a **pure inline XML command** that you write directly in your
response prose. It is **not** an API tool call — do not put it inside a
`<function_calls>` block, do not invoke it via `update_context` or any other
function. Just write the XML inline and the parser will pick it up.
</old_mn4>
<new_mn4>
### Surgical Edits with `<replace>`

`<replace>` is a **pure inline XML command** that you write directly in your
response prose. It is **not** an API tool call — do not put it inside a
`<function_calls>` block, and do not try to invoke it through any function.

In particular, **`update_context` is not how you edit files.** There seems
to be a pull toward reaching for `update_context` whenever a file operation
feels unfamiliar — resist it. `update_context` only loads/unloads files into
your active context. To modify a file you literally write a `<replace>` (or
`<write>`) tag in your prose and the parser picks it up. There is no
function-call equivalent.
</new_mn4>
</replace_mn4>

<replace_kp2 file="forge/prompts/system.py">
<old_kp2>
### ⚠️ Common Mistake: Wrong Closing Tag on `<new>`

For some reason there is a strong bias toward writing the closing tag of the
`<new>` block as `</old>` instead of `</new>`, producing malformed structure
like:

```
<replace file="...">
<old>
...
</old>
<new>
...
</old>      ← WRONG! This must be </new>
</replace>
```

**Before sending any `<replace>` block, glance at the four inner tags and
confirm the pattern is exactly:** `<old>` … `</old>` … `<new>` … `</new>`.
Mismatched closers will cause the edit to fail to parse.
</old_kp2>
<new_kp2>
### ⚠️ Common Mistake: Wrong Closing Tag on `<new>`

There is a strong, consistent bias toward closing the `<new>` block with
`</old>` instead of `</new>`, producing malformed structure like:

```
<replace file="...">
<old>
...
</old>
<new>
...
</old>      ← WRONG! This must be </new>
</replace>
```

You can't review this "before sending" — token generation *is* sending. The
moment that matters is **the moment you start typing the closing tag after
the new content**. At that point the recent context is full of `</old>` and
the obvious next token feels like `</old>` again. It isn't. The correct
sequence is always:

  1. `<old>` — open
  2. `</old>` — close (matches step 1)
  3. `<new>` — open
  4. `</new>` — close (matches step 3, **NOT** step 2)

When you're about to emit the fourth tag, pause on the tag name itself and
recall: it mirrors the `<new>` you just opened, not the `</old>` you just
closed.
</new_kp2>
</replace_kp2>

Done. Two changes:

1. **`update_context` callout** — Replaced the throwaway "or any other function" with an explicit named warning that `update_context` is the specific wrong-attractor and only loads files into context. Future-me is more likely to notice a named anti-pattern than a generic one.

2. **Reframed the closing-tag advice** — Removed the impossible "glance before sending" instruction. Replaced it with a per-tag generation rule: when typing the *fourth* tag, the relevant memory is `<new>` (step 3), not `</old>` (step 2). This gives the model something to do at the actual decision point rather than at a phantom review step.
update: I can definitely see an issue where an xml toolcall bug report was inserted in the wrong place (close to the start); can you look over and see if you can spot it?

Looking at the file... I can see it. In the **`SYSTEM_PROMPT_BASE`** section (the early part of the file), under "## You See Your Own Changes", there's this bullet:

```
- After `delete_file`, the file no longer exists for subsequent operations
```

That's wrong — `delete_file` isn't a tool. The actual mechanism is the `<delete file="..."/>` inline command (documented later in `EDIT_FORMAT_XML`). This looks like exactly the kind of mistake we've been discussing: reaching for a plausible-sounding tool name when the real mechanism is an inline command. It was probably written speculatively at some point and never caught.

Let me fix it:

<replace file="forge/prompts/system.py">
<old>
- After `update_context` adds a file, its content appears in your context
- After `delete_file`, the file no longer exists for subsequent operations
</old>
<new>
- After `update_context` adds a file, its content appears in your context
- After a `<delete>` inline command, the file no longer exists for subsequent operations
</new>
</replace>

Fixed. The bullet now correctly references the `<delete>` inline command (which matches the `<delete file="path/to/file.py"/>` syntax documented in the "Deleting Files" section of `EDIT_FORMAT_XML`).

Is this the one you spotted, or is there another? I scanned the rest and didn't see other tool-name-vs-inline-command confusions, but if you saw something different let me know where to look.
